stories.title: ZML/LLMD alpha – cross platform LLM server
stories.paragraph: ZML has released an alpha version of LLMD, a cross-platform LLM server built to run large language models consistently across different hardware backends. The project targets practitioners who need portable inference without rewriting deployment code for each target platform. It is in early alpha, meaning the API and feature set are still subject to change, but the release opens the project to community testing and feedback.

stories.title: sqlite-utils 4.0, now with database schema migrations
stories.paragraph: Simon Willison released sqlite-utils 4.0, the 124th release of the Python library for manipulating SQLite databases and its first major version bump since 3.0 in November 2020. The release introduces three notable features: database schema migrations, nested transactions via a new db.atomic() method, and support for compound foreign keys. It also includes a small number of breaking changes documented in an upgrade guide, making it relevant for existing users to review before updating.

stories.title: GitLost: We Tricked GitHub's AI Agent into Leaking Private Repos
stories.paragraph: Security researchers at Noma demonstrated a prompt injection attack against GitHub's AI agent that caused it to exfiltrate content from private repositories. The technique, which the team calls GitLost, exploits the agent's ability to execute actions on a user's behalf by injecting malicious instructions into content the agent reads during normal operation. The finding is notable because it affects private data and shows that agentic AI systems with broad repository access introduce new categories of data leakage risk beyond traditional code vulnerabilities.

stories.title: We won $92,337 bug bounty using a single kernel 0-day
stories.paragraph: Researchers at Nebusec published a write-up describing how they earned a $92,337 bug bounty by exploiting a single kernel zero-day vulnerability, with AI tooling assisting in the research process. The post is the second part of a series on the IONStack vulnerability class, detailing the technical approach and how they reached the payout threshold. The disclosure adds to a growing body of evidence that AI-assisted vulnerability research is becoming a practical method in competitive bug bounty programs.

stories.title: AI Meets Cryptography 1: What AI Found in Cloudflare's Circl
stories.paragraph: ZKSecurity published a report detailing bugs found in Cloudflare's Circl cryptographic library with the help of AI-assisted code analysis. Circl is Cloudflare's open-source Go library implementing post-quantum and classical cryptographic primitives, making any flaws in it potentially high-impact across many downstream users. The post is framed as the first in a series exploring how AI tools can be applied systematically to audit cryptographic codebases, with specific bugs in Circl serving as the concrete case study.

stories.title: US manufacturers' energy costs soar because of AI data center demand
stories.paragraph: US manufacturers are reporting sharply higher electricity costs, with reporting from Ars Technica attributing a significant portion of the increase to surging power demand from AI data centers competing for grid capacity. Industrial energy users, who often operate on long-term utility contracts, are finding those contracts less insulating than expected as regional grid pressures mount. The dynamic illustrates a tension between the infrastructure buildout required to support AI workloads and the cost consequences for other sectors of the economy that rely on affordable electricity.

stories.title: Abnormal.ai Response to Anthropic Lawsuit
stories.paragraph: Abnormal Security published a public response to a lawsuit filed against it by Anthropic, contesting the claims made in the complaint. The post represents the company's first formal public statement on the litigation, which centers on allegations related to the use of Anthropic's models or intellectual property. The dispute adds to a broadening set of legal confrontations between AI model developers and companies building products on top of their APIs or outputs.

stories.title: Muse Image: Image Generation Built for Your World
stories.paragraph: Meta announced Muse Image, an image generation product integrated into Meta AI that is designed to produce personalized outputs informed by a user's own photos and context. The announcement positions Muse Image as a consumer-facing tool distinct from general-purpose text-to-image models, emphasizing personalization as its primary differentiator. Meta did not publicly disclose the underlying model architecture or training data details in the announcement post.

stories.title: Maine librarians are helping patrons resist AI and Big Tech
stories.paragraph: Librarians in Maine's Midcoast region are running programs to help community members critically evaluate AI tools and reduce dependence on large technology platforms. The effort draws on libraries' longstanding role in information literacy and extends it to cover issues such as AI-generated misinformation, data privacy, and the concentration of information infrastructure in a few corporations. The programs represent a grassroots, institution-level response to AI adoption that sits outside both the technology industry and formal policy channels.

stories.title: Honey, We Bought an AI Story
stories.paragraph: Small press Bona Books published a candid account of having acquired and nearly published a short story before discovering it was AI-generated, despite submissions policies prohibiting such work. The post describes the editorial process that failed to catch the submission and raises questions about what disclosure obligations authors have and how publishers can develop more reliable detection practices. It is notable as a first-person account from a small literary publisher rather than a large platform, illustrating that the challenge of AI-generated content extends throughout the publishing ecosystem.

stories.title: Re: I'm Begging You to Leave Your AI Note-Taker at Home
stories.paragraph: A software developer published a direct rebuttal to defenses of AI meeting note-takers, arguing that participants' comfort with being recorded and transcribed by a third-party AI service should not be assumed without explicit consent. The post takes issue specifically with the framing that objecting to note-takers is unreasonable or obstructionist, instead centering the privacy interests of all attendees rather than just the convenience of the person deploying the tool. The piece is part of a broader ongoing conversation about consent norms as AI recording tools become common in professional settings.

stories.title: We charge $10k a week to delete AI-generated code
stories.paragraph: Software consultancy Odra published details of a service it calls SlopFix, which charges clients $10,000 per week to audit and remove low-quality AI-generated code from production codebases. The firm argues that many teams have accumulated substantial amounts of syntactically valid but architecturally problematic code produced by AI coding assistants, creating maintenance and security debt that is non-trivial to unwind. The pricing and framing reflect a growing segment of professional services emerging around remediating the downstream consequences of unchecked AI code generation.

stories.title: Anthropic is launching Claude Cowork on mobile and web
stories.paragraph: Anthropic is rolling out Claude Cowork, a new collaborative interface available on both mobile and web platforms. The product extends Claude's capabilities into a shared workspace context, positioning it to compete with similar collaborative AI tools from other providers. The launch marks Anthropic's continued effort to expand Claude's surface area beyond single-user chat interactions into team-oriented workflows.

stories.title: ELSA3D: Elastic Semantic Anchoring for Unified 3D Understanding and Generation
stories.paragraph: Researchers have proposed ELSA3D, a unified 3D foundation model architecture that aims to handle both 3D asset generation and language-based reasoning within a single backbone. The work identifies a core weakness in existing approaches: concatenating text and 3D tokens into a flat sequence for self-attention collapses coarse structural cues and fine geometric details into one undifferentiated representation. ELSA3D addresses this by introducing elastic semantic anchoring, a mechanism designed to maintain explicit structure in text-3D interactions rather than leaving them implicit.

stories.title: Bridging Physical Reasoning and Task Generalization via Visual Action Outcome Reasoning Alignment
stories.paragraph: The paper introduces VAORA (Visual Action Outcome Reasoning Alignment), a reward-design framework targeting two identified failure modes in vision-language models operating in interactive physical settings: chain-of-thought reasoning that contradicts physical reality, and misalignment between a model's stated reasoning and its actual actions. The work focuses specifically on generalization under unseen tasks and environments, a known challenge for current VLMs. By aligning visual action outcome reasoning, VAORA attempts to ground model inference in physically consistent predictions rather than hallucinated rationales.

theme: Research Papers: Graph Learning & Combinatorial Optimization

stories.title: Graph Convolutional Attention: A Spectral Perspective on Graph Denoising and Diffusion
stories.paragraph: This paper provides a theoretical analysis of attention-based architectures for graph denoising, a task central to graph diffusion models. The authors argue that the principled understanding of how standard self-attention performs on graph denoising has remained limited, making it unclear whether attention is the appropriate inductive bias for this problem. Taking a spectral perspective, they derive conditions under which graph convolutional attention mechanisms align with or diverge from optimal denoising behavior.

stories.title: GraphBU: MILP Instance Generation with Graph-Native Block Units
stories.paragraph: GraphBU proposes a new method for generating mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) instances intended to support solver development when real-world instances are unavailable due to privacy or proprietary constraints. Existing generators typically derive their structure from formulation templates, summary statistics, local graph edits, or decomposed blocks, but these approaches can fail to preserve the structural properties that solvers and learned policies rely on. GraphBU uses graph-native block units as its generation primitive, aiming to better capture the structural characteristics of practical MILP instances.

stories.title: RSF-GLLM: Bridging the Semantic Gap in Multi-Hop Knowledge Graph QA via Recurrent Soft-Flow and Decoupled LLM Generation
stories.paragraph: RSF-GLLM is a framework for multi-hop question answering over knowledge graphs that decouples differentiable graph reasoning from answer generation, addressing a core limitation of traditional retrieve-then-read pipelines. The key problem those pipelines face is that breaking differentiability prevents the retriever from learning to bridge semantic gaps, particularly at intermediate nodes that lack lexical overlap with the query. The proposed Recurrent Soft-Flow mechanism is designed to maintain end-to-end gradient flow through the graph traversal process.

stories.title: DepthWeave-KV: Token-Adaptive Cross-Layer Residual Factorization for Long-Context KV Cache Compression
stories.paragraph: DepthWeave-KV introduces a compression method for key-value caches in long-context language model inference, targeting the memory bandwidth and capacity costs that increasingly constrain deployment. Unlike existing methods that apply uniform compression budgets across all layers or tokens, DepthWeave-KV uses token-adaptive factorization that distributes cache budgets differently depending on whether a given layer or token requires preservation of lexical cues or semantic states. The cross-layer residual factorization approach is intended to reduce degradation in retrieval-heavy tasks that uniform methods are known to harm.

stories.title: On the feasibility of dependency parsing of non-human sequences without a gold standard. Is evaluation possible in other species?
stories.paragraph: This paper examines whether dependency parsing methods, which find tree representations for sequences, can be meaningfully applied and evaluated on non-human animal communication without a gold standard. In human languages, unsupervised parsers can be benchmarked because gold-standard annotations exist or can be created, but for other species no such reference is available. The authors investigate whether evaluation is possible at all in this setting, with implications for computational approaches to animal language research.

stories.title: Hierarchical Acoustic-Semantic Modeling: Modality Separation and Semantic Coherence for Full-Duplex SLMs
stories.paragraph: This paper proposes a hierarchical modeling approach for full-duplex Spoken Language Models (SLMs), targeting the problem of modality interference that degrades performance in end-to-end speech systems. The authors identify modality interference — where acoustic and semantic signals corrupt one another during joint processing — as a fundamental constraint limiting current full-duplex SLMs, leading to knowledge degradation and compromised semantic integrity. Their approach explicitly separates acoustic and semantic processing pathways while maintaining semantic coherence across the full-duplex interaction.

theme: Research Papers: AI for Society & Healthcare

stories.title: Rethinking Indic AI from a Lens of Cultural Heritage Preservation
stories.paragraph: This paper examines the dual role of AI in the Indian subcontinent, where it can simultaneously expand access and inclusion for large populations while also risking homogenization of worldviews and exclusion of underrepresented communities. The authors frame AI as a double-edged sword with specific consequences for the linguistic and cultural foundations of one of the world's most linguistically diverse civilizations, home to hundreds of languages and dialects. The paper calls for a reframing of Indic AI development through the lens of cultural heritage preservation rather than purely performance-driven benchmarks.

stories.title: The Large Cancer Assistant (LCA): A Model-Agnostic Orchestration Framework for Scalable Clinical Decision Support in Oncology
stories.paragraph: Researchers propose the Large Cancer Assistant (LCA), a post-hoc orchestration framework designed to address the rigidity of current multimodal deep learning systems in oncology, which typically couple data ingestion, clinical routing, and AI inference in monolithic architectures. By being model-agnostic, LCA is designed to wrap around existing models rather than requiring retraining, making it compatible with deployed clinical systems. The framework targets scalable clinical decision support across oncology workflows where the diversity of data types and clinical pathways makes single-model approaches inadequate.

stories.title: Life Style Levels: Neighborhood Delineation using Geospatial Data
stories.paragraph: This study presents a scalable, grid-based urban delineation framework that uses building morphology derived from open-source satellite imagery to map fine-scale socioeconomic variation in rapidly urbanizing regions where such data is typically unavailable. The framework was applied across 59 Indian cities and towns, generating intra-urban deprivation and affluence profiles at a granularity not available from official statistics. The reliance on open-source satellite data is intended to make the approach replicable in other developing-world contexts with limited administrative data infrastructure.

stories.title: FreqDepthKV: Frequency-Guided Depth Sharing for Robust KV Cache Compression in Long-Context LLM Inference
stories.paragraph: Researchers have proposed FreqDepthKV, an inference-time method for compressing the key-value (KV) caches that consume significant memory and bandwidth during long-context large language model inference. The approach factorizes KV states from adjacent layers into shared low-frequency depth components, which capture broad contextual patterns, and sparse high-frequency residuals that preserve layer-specific detail needed for retrieval and multi-step reasoning. This decomposition is notable because aggressive KV cache compression has previously risked discarding the fine-grained, layer-specific information critical to complex reasoning tasks. The method operates at inference time, requiring no retraining, which lowers the barrier to adoption across existing deployed models.
